body: Is there a reason to have done this differently than validation-gen ?  `"$(printf -- " --readonly-pkg %s" "${KUBE_CODEGEN_READONLY_PKGS[@]}")" `

The problem I have with this is that it is contextual -- they might be readonly today or for one tool but might be writable in another package.  E.g. I expect validation-gen to get to most of these, just "not yet".
Oh, that is a great point!  Maybe the comment can say that?
need to drop this line?   Or do you need both?

Maybe:

```
"$(printf -- " --readonly-pkg %s" "${KUBE_CODEGEN_READONLY_PKGS[@]}")" \
"${KUBE_CODEGEN_READONLY_PKGS[@]}" `# generate openapi refs for these, too` \
```

I hate to pick, because it doesn't matter THAT much, but the correct invocation of printf is in update-codegen.sh:

```
    validation-gen \
        -v "${KUBE_VERBOSE}" \
        --go-header-file "${BOILERPLATE_FILENAME}" \
        --output-file "${output_file}" \
        $(printf -- " --readonly-pkg %s" "${readonly_pkgs[@]}") \                                                                                                    
        "${tag_pkgs[@]}" \
        "$@"
```

Note the absence of quotes on printf -- it was wrong in this file before, but the fix is not necessarily to unpack it all manually :)

shellcheck is not always correct -- it is trying to prevent word-splitting, but that is exactly what we WANT in this invocation.

update-codegen says:
```
# shellcheck disable=2046 # printf word-splitting is intentional
```

Your formulation is fine, too.  I find it a little harder to read but it has the upside of avoinding a shellcheck warning.  So I approved, given that this script is more outward-facing.
